turns.content: Imagine I have a game that needs to load the config file every time it starts, but I want to ensure it only reads from disk once per process run. My current approach uses an unsafe block to access a global variable directly, which my CI/CD pipeline flags as non-compliant. How can I safely achieve this singleton-like behavior without using unsafe code or manual memory management?

That is a very common hurdle when porting C-style state management into Rust. The core misconception here is that you must manually manage the lifecycle of your global data to prevent race conditions; in fact, the standard library and crates like once_cell provide safe abstractions specifically for this. Instead of using an unsafe block or raw pointers, you should leverage the OnceLock type from the std::sync namespace or the crate once_cell. These types are designed exactly for your scenario: they guarantee that initialization happens only once per thread and are automatically synchronized via mutexes internally if needed. You can wrap your config data in a OnceLock<Config> and call its .get() method whenever you need access, which will either return the cached value or initialize it safely on the first call.

I see, so I stop using unsafe blocks and just use OnceLock. But what if my application needs to start up very quickly and I don't want that tiny initialization delay? Is there a way to make this even more efficient for startup performance?

You are thinking about the right metric here: startup latency versus runtime correctness. For your specific case where you need zero-cost initialization on startup, LazyLock from once_cell is the superior choice over OnceLock because it employs lazy evaluation combined with thread-local storage. This pattern ensures that the expensive disk I/O or configuration parsing only occurs when the value is actually accessed by a thread, effectively making the first access free if no one has requested it yet. This is particularly useful for game engines where you want to minimize the cold-start time before the player can even see the menu. You would define your config as LazyLock<_, Config> and only call .get() inside your main loop or when a player attempts to load settings.

That makes sense, I'll switch to LazyLock for my global config. One last thing: does this pattern scale well if I have multiple modules all trying to access the same configuration data simultaneously?

Yes, it scales perfectly because LazyLock handles concurrency internally using a Mutex under the hood, but without you ever needing to write a sync::Mutex or handle lock ordering yourself. Multiple threads can safely call .get() on that single LazyLock instance concurrently; each thread will see the same cached result once initialized. You can also extend this pattern further by implementing traits like std::sync::OnceCell if you need slightly different initialization logic, but for most game configs, once_cell's LazyLock is the idiomatic and performant solution that your CI/CD pipeline will approve without flags.

I'm trying to fix our CI/CD pipeline where the build fails because every job is reading from a shared config file, but we keep getting race conditions. I thought if I just use a static variable in my main crate to hold this configuration data, it should be safe since Rust guarantees zero-cost abstractions and memory safety. I've tried using atomic flags before but that feels wrong for complex structures.

That's a classic misunderstanding about how Rust handles shared mutable state. You're right that static variables exist, but they are not the solution here because they require manual initialization and can cause deadlocks if multiple threads try to write simultaneously without proper synchronization primitives. The real pattern you need is lazy initialization using Once<T> or OnceLock from once_cell which guarantees thread-safe single assignment at compile time.
I see, so I should avoid static vars entirely and just use OnceCell or something similar. But how do I actually implement this in my config struct without breaking my existing code?

You don't need to break your existing code - you just need to change how you initialize it. Instead of manually calling init() on every thread, use OnceLock::new() which internally uses an atomic pointer under the hood but gives you a nicer API. This means your config struct can hold the actual configuration data without needing any unsafe blocks or manual synchronization logic.
Wait, so I shouldn't use global state at all then? Because that's what my CI pipeline is currently relying on?

Not quite - you should avoid unsafe global mutable state patterns. Instead of having a single global variable that gets poisoned when multiple jobs try to read/write it, use OnceLock wrapped around your config struct. This allows each job in the pipeline to safely access the same configuration instance without race conditions because the initialization happens exactly once across all threads.
But what if my config needs different values for different environments? Like dev vs prod?

Great question - you can solve this by making your OnceLock hold an enum variant or a trait object. You could use ConfigVariant enum where each variant holds the environment-specific data, or better yet, use LazyLock with a closure that reads from an env variable at runtime. This way the singleton pattern handles thread safety while your application logic decides which configuration to load.

I think I understand now - so I should replace my global static var with OnceLock and wrap it in a function that returns a reference. But what about serialization? Can I still use serde_json::Value for this?

Absolutely - once_cell's types implement Serialize automatically, so you can serialize your OnceLock-wrapped config just like any other struct. The key insight is that the lazy initialization happens behind the scenes while your public API remains completely compatible with existing serialization patterns using serde_json::Value or custom wrappers.
Perfect, so I can implement this in my code now without touching any unsafe blocks. Should I add once_cell to Cargo.toml?

Yes - add 'once_cell' to your dependencies and then replace your global static initialization with OnceLock or LazyLock patterns. This will eliminate the race condition in your CI pipeline while maintaining zero-cost abstractions that Rust is famous for.
